summary: The author developed a custom neural network with separate 1D-CNN branches for each sensor modality to classify gestures from wearable sensor data. Key preprocessing steps included aligning left-handed subject data by flipping signs and canceling true north orientation, and detecting/correcting two subjects who wore devices upside down. The model used a U-Net to estimate gesture segments for targeted temporal pooling and combined multiple prediction heads with weighted cross-entropy loss. Final predictions were generated by averaging outputs from 100 models trained with different random seeds.
extensive_summary:
## Solution Overview
The author built a custom neural network architecture featuring separate 1D-CNN branches for each sensor modality, followed by MLP layers. A critical design choice was using grouped convolutions for the first layer in each 1D-CNN block. Instead of global average pooling, the model pooled features separately over estimated gesture and non-gesture segments, concatenated them, and fed them into the MLP. Gesture segments were pre-estimated using a simple U-Net and added as auxiliary inputs.

## Preprocessing & Data Handling
A major focus was aligning sensor data across subjects. For left-handed participants, the author flipped the x-component of acceleration and the y/z components of rotation after canceling out the subject's facing direction relative to true north. Two subjects were identified as wearing their devices upside down (180° z-axis rotation) by analyzing acc_x and acc_y histograms; their data was corrected by flipping x/y signs, applying z-axis rotations, and swapping sensor channels. Additional cleaning included replacing THM values below 20°C with nulls (filled with 0 after standardization) and converting TOF values of -1 to 255. Only the last 75 frames of each sequence were used as input.

## Feature Engineering & Architecture Details
The `derived_acc` block combined linear acceleration (gravity removed) and global acceleration. `rotvec_diff` served as an angular velocity proxy. For TOF sensors, spatial convolution was applied on an 8×8 grid, followed by three rounds of stride-2 max pooling to collapse spatial dimensions into 32 channels per sensor (160 total), which were then processed temporally. The network produced multiple prediction heads: all-input, IMU-only, and per-block predictions, combined with weighted cross-entropy loss. An auxiliary orientation prediction (4 classes) was added with a 0.5 weight. During inference, IMU-only predictions were used if >50% of TOF values were missing.

## Training & Ensembling
Two nearly identical networks (differing only in input blocks) were trained 50 times each on the full dataset with different random seeds, resulting in 100 models. Outputs were averaged for the final submission. The author used RAdamScheduleFree as the optimizer and did not apply any post-processing or threshold tuning.
competition_flow: Raw sensor sequences are preprocessed to align left-handed data and correct upside-down device placements, then passed through a custom 1D-CNN and U-Net architecture to extract modality-specific features and gesture segments, followed by training 100 models via seed averaging and output ensemble averaging for submission.
data_processing: Extracted the last 75 frames of each sequence
Aligned left-handed data by flipping acceleration/rotation signs and canceling true north facing direction
Corrected upside-down devices (180° z-axis rotation) by flipping x/y signs, rotating z, and swapping sensor channels
Replaced THM values below 20°C with nulls (filled with 0 after standardization)
features_engineering: TOF spatial convolution on an 8×8 grid with stride-2 max pooling to produce 160 channels
U-Net estimated gesture segments for targeted temporal pooling
loss_functions: Cross-entropy loss for gesture classification (18 classes)
Auxiliary orientation prediction loss (4 classes, weighted at 0.5)
Weighted combination of all-input (1/4), IMU-only (1/4), and six block-specific predictions (1/12 each)
ensembling: Averaged outputs of 100 neural networks (two nearly identical architectures trained 50 times each with different random seeds on the full dataset); no post-processing or threshold tuning applied.
insights: Aligning left-handed subject data distributions was critical for model accuracy.
Detecting and correcting upside-down device placement significantly improved private leaderboard performance.
Using separate temporal pooling over gesture and non-gesture segments improved feature extraction.
Excluding absolute orientation features may be necessary for real-world deployment where facing directions vary.
critical_findings: Two subjects wore devices upside down (180° z-axis rotation), detected via flipped acc_x/y histograms; correcting them boosted the private score by +0.013.
Left-handed rotation data required canceling the subject's true north facing direction before flipping y/z components.
EMA improved single CV results but its benefits vanished when combined with seed averaging.
what_did_not_work: Data augmentation and mix-up failed to improve performance.
EMA's improvements disappeared when combined with seed averaging.

other_solution_refs.description: Discussion link referenced for global acceleration calculation
